subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 4! In this unit you will grow into a confident, careful calculator who can work with very large numbers, with negative numbers, and with the very first ideas of algebra. You use addition and subtraction every single day, often without noticing. When Amina works out the change from a 500-shilling note at the Hargeisa market, when a pilot flying out of Mogadishu adds up the distance to Nairobi and back, or when Yusuf checks whether the temperature has risen or fallen overnight, they are all adding and subtracting.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the standardized Cambridge Stage 6 workbook source rows for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, copy every worked example onto paper and try it yourself, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, to explain a step in a fresh way, or to check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

Why does this matter so much? Numbers describe the whole world around you. Traders use large numbers to add up a month of sales. Weather stations use negative numbers to record cold nights on the high plateau near Hargeisa. Banks use negative numbers when an account is overdrawn, and engineers use them for depths below sea level. Algebra – the art of using letters for unknown numbers – is the language that scientists, builders and computer programmers all speak. When you become confident with these ideas, you are gaining tools you will use for the rest of your life, and you are building the strong foundation you need for the harder mathematics ahead.

As you work, keep a pencil and plenty of paper beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must do it. Line up the digits, draw the number lines, and say the answers out loud. If you get something wrong, that is not a failure; it is simply a signpost pointing to exactly what to practise next. Every mathematician began where you are now, learning these very same ideas one careful step at a time.
Estimate sums and differences of large numbers by rounding to a sensible place value.
Add and subtract large whole numbers accurately using column methods.
Check calculations using estimation and inverse operations.
Understand and compare positive and negative numbers on a number line.
Add and subtract positive and negative integers.
Find the difference between two numbers, including negative ones.
Use letters to represent unknown numbers and write simple expressions.

concepts.title: Estimating with Large Numbers
concepts.explanation: Before you work out any calculation with large numbers, you should estimate the answer first. An estimate is a quick, sensible guess found by rounding the numbers to tidy values you can add or subtract in your head. Estimating first is a habit that skilled mathematicians keep for life, because it warns you at once if your exact answer goes badly wrong. To estimate, round each number to a convenient place value – often the nearest thousand or the nearest ten thousand. Then do the easy calculation with the rounded…
concepts.example: Example: Estimate 34,405 + 90,253. Round 34,405 to 30,000 and 90,253 to 90,000. The estimate is 30,000 + 90,000 = 120,000. (The exact answer is 124,658, so our estimate is close and sensible.)

concepts.title: Written Methods and Checking Your Work
concepts.explanation: Once you have an estimate, you work out the exact answer using a written column method. Line the digits up carefully by place value – ones under ones, tens under tens, and so on – so that nothing drifts out of place. This tidy lining-up is the single most useful habit for accurate work with large numbers. When you add, start at the ones on the right and work left, carrying a ten into the next column whenever a column total reaches ten or more. When you subtract, again start on the right, and borrow (exchange) from…
concepts.example: After every calculation, check your answer in two ways. First, compare it to your estimate: if they are close, that is a good sign. Second, use the inverse operation – the operation that undoes the one you did. Addition…

concepts.title: Positive and Negative Numbers
concepts.explanation: So far you have worked mostly with numbers greater than zero. Now we meet the numbers below zero. Positive numbers are greater than zero and may be written with or without a plus sign: +5 or simply 5. Negative numbers are less than zero and are always written with a minus sign: −5, −10, −100. Zero itself is neither positive nor negative – it is the dividing point between the two. The best picture for these numbers is the number line. Imagine a straight road with zero in the middle. To the right of zero lie the…

concepts.title: Adding and Subtracting with Negative Numbers
concepts.explanation: The number line does more than order numbers – it also shows you how to add and subtract them. Think of every calculation as a journey along the line. You start at the first number, then make a move that the calculation tells you. For adding, the rules are: adding a positive number means moving right; adding a negative number means moving left.
concepts.example: −3 + 5: start at −3, move 5 places right, and land on 2.

concepts.title: Using Letters to Represent Numbers
concepts.explanation: Sometimes we do not yet know the exact value of a number, but we still want to work with it. In algebra we use a letter to stand for that unknown number. This is one of the most powerful ideas in all of mathematics, and you are meeting it here for the first time. Two words help you read algebra clearly. A variable is a letter that can represent different numbers – in the expression d + 4, the letter d might be 1, 2, 3 or any value. A constant is a number that stays the same – in d + 4, the 4 is always 4. An…
concepts.example: Example: In a board game you move '4 more than the dice score'. If d is the dice score, the number of spaces you move is the expression d + 4. When the dice shows 3, you move d + 4 = 3 + 4 = 7 spaces. When it shows 6,…

concepts.title: Simple Equations and Perimeter Expressions
concepts.explanation: An equation is a number sentence that says two things are equal, with an equals sign between them, such as x + 9 = 17. Solving an equation means finding the value of the letter that makes the sentence true. The key tool is the inverse operation – whatever has been done to the letter, you undo. Example: Solve x + 9 = 17. The letter has had 9 added to it, so to undo that we subtract 9 from both sides: x = 17 − 9 = 8. Check by substituting: 8 + 9 = 17. ✓ Example: Solve y − 6 = 12. Here 6 has been subtracted, so we…
concepts.example: Equations can also come from stories. Two puzzle pieces problem: Hassan places x pieces and Yusuf places y pieces of a 9-piece puzzle, so x + y = 9. If Hassan places 4, then 4 + y = 9, so y = 5. If Hassan places 6, then…

methods.title: How to calculate with large numbers
methods.example: Work out 56,789 + 23,456, using estimation to check.
steps: Estimate first by rounding to a sensible place value.
Line the digits up by place value in neat columns.
Add or subtract from the right, carrying or borrowing as needed.
Check against your estimate and with the inverse operation.

methods.title: How to add or subtract integers
steps: Start at the first number on a number line.
Adding moves you: right for a positive, left for a negative.
Subtracting moves you: left for a positive, right for a negative.
Read off the number where you land.

methods.title: How to work with a letter
methods.example: A library has 234,567 books. It adds 45,678 new books and removes 12,345 damaged ones. How many books remain?
steps: Decide what the letter stands for.
Write the expression or equation from the words.
Substitute known values, or undo with inverse operations to solve.
Check your answer makes sense in the story.

methods.title: Ordering integers
steps: Solution: Picture the number line.
The most negative comes first, then up towards the positives: −7 < −3 < −1 < 0 < 5 < 8.
Remember that −7 is smaller than −3 because it sits further left.

methods.title: Adding and subtracting integers
steps: Solution: (a) Start at −8 and move 5 right: −8 + 5 = −3.
(b) Start at −3 and move 7 left: −3 − 7 = −10.
Draw the moves on a number line if it helps you see them.

methods.title: A temperature difference
methods.example: Moscow is −15°C and London is 8°C. What is the temperature difference?
steps: Solution: Subtract the smaller from the larger: 8 − (−15) = 8 + 15 = 23°C.
Subtracting a negative became adding a positive.
The difference is a positive amount – London is 23 degrees warmer.

workedExamples.title: Estimate then calculate
workedExamples.prompt: Work out 56,789 + 23,456, using estimation to check.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Estimate first: round to 57,000 + 23,000 = 80,000. Now add exactly in columns: 56,789 + 23,456 = 80,245. The exact answer is very close to the estimate of 80,000, so it is reasonable. ✓

workedExamples.title: Find and fix an error
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Check with the inverse operation: 31,180 + 5,724 = 36,904, which is not 36,804, so the answer is wrong. Redoing the subtraction carefully gives 36,804 − 5,724 = 31,080. Now 31,080 + 5,724 = 36,804. ✓ The mistake was a small borrowing slip.

workedExamples.title: A multi-step whole-number story
workedExamples.prompt: A library has 234,567 books. It adds 45,678 new books and removes 12,345 damaged ones. How many books remain?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Add the new books, then subtract the damaged ones. 234,567 + 45,678 = 280,245. Then 280,245 − 12,345 = 267,900 books. Estimate check: 235,000 + 46,000 − 12,000 = 269,000, which is close. ✓

workedExamples.title: Ordering integers
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Picture the number line. The most negative comes first, then up towards the positives: −7 < −3 < −1 < 0 < 5 < 8. Remember that −7 is smaller than −3 because it sits further left.

workedExamples.title: Adding and subtracting integers
workedExamples.solution: Solution: (a) Start at −8 and move 5 right: −8 + 5 = −3. (b) Start at −3 and move 7 left: −3 − 7 = −10. Draw the moves on a number line if it helps you see them.

workedExamples.title: A temperature difference
workedExamples.prompt: Moscow is −15°C and London is 8°C. What is the temperature difference?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Subtract the smaller from the larger: 8 − (−15) = 8 + 15 = 23°C. Subtracting a negative became adding a positive. The difference is a positive amount – London is 23 degrees warmer.

workedExamples.title: Substituting into an expression
workedExamples.prompt: Evaluate a + 8 and 12 − a when a = 6.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Replace a with 6 each time. a + 8 = 6 + 8 = 14. 12 − a = 12 − 6 = 6. Substitution means swapping the letter for its value, then calculating.

workedExamples.title: Solving an equation
workedExamples.solution: Solution: For x + 9 = 17, subtract 9 from both sides: x = 17 − 9 = 8. For y − 6 = 12, add 6 to both sides: y = 12 + 6 = 18. Always undo with the inverse operation, and check by substituting back.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 9
workedExamples.prompt: Evaluate n + 8 when: a) n = 5 b) n = 0 c) n = −3.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 11
workedExamples.prompt: Find the difference between: a) 8 and −3 b) −5 and −12.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 12
workedExamples.prompt: A company's quarter results are: profit 234,567; loss 123,456; profit 345,678; loss 89,234. Writing losses as negatives, find the year's total and say if it is a profit or loss.

activities.title: Build a Walk-On Number Line
activities.materials: chalk or paper cards, and a bit of floor space.
steps: Mark a number line on the ground from −10 to +10 with 0 in the middle.
Stand on a starting number. For 'add', step right; for 'subtract', step left.
Act out −3 + 5, 4 − 8 and −5 − (−3) by walking the moves.
Say your answer out loud each time and check it against a friend's.
Walking the moves turns the number-line rules into something your body remembers.

activities.title: The Thermometer Game
activities.materials: paper, pencil, and two dice (or number cards).
steps: Draw a vertical thermometer scale from −10°C to +10°C.
Start at a temperature such as −4°C.
Roll a die and flip a coin: heads means a rise, tails means a fall of that many degrees.
Move up or down the scale and record the new temperature after each roll.
This makes adding and subtracting negatives feel as natural as watching the weather.

activities.title: Estimation Challenge at the Market
activities.materials: prices or quantities from a real or pretend market (using shillings).
steps: Pick four items with prices in the thousands, such as 12,345 and 8,967 shillings.
Estimate the total by rounding each price to the nearest thousand.
Now add the exact prices with a written method.
Compare your estimate to the exact total – how close were you?

activities.title: Bank Balance Diary (Family Task)
activities.materials: paper and pencil, and a starting balance of your choice.
steps: Start a pretend account with 100 shillings.
Take turns with a family member to invent deposits and withdrawals.
Record the running balance after each transaction, using negative numbers when overdrawn.
At the end, check your final balance by adding all deposits and subtracting all withdrawals.

activities.title: Letters Everywhere (Expression Hunt)
steps: Choose a letter, such as d for the number of dates in one bag.
Write expressions for real situations: two more than a bag is d + 2; three fewer is d − 3.
Pick three values for d and substitute them into each expression.
Challenge a family member to make up a situation for you to turn into an expression.

activities.title: Consecutive Numbers Investigation
steps: Pick any three numbers in a row, such as 7, 8, 9, and add them.
Try several more triples and look for a pattern between the sum and the middle number.
Now let the first number be n and write the three numbers and their sum as expressions.
Use your expression to predict the sum of 20, 21, 22 before adding to check.

After your activities, talk or write about these questions: Which activity helped you understand negative numbers best? What was the trickiest part of writing expressions with letters? What would you like more practice with? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Estimation Rule
rules.text: Round each number to a tidy place value, then calculate. 34,405 + 90,253 is about 30,000 + 90,000 = 120,000. Use the estimate to check your exact answer is sensible.

rules.title: Number-Line Rule
rules.text: Add a positive: move right. Add a negative: move left. Subtract a positive: move left. Subtract a negative: move right. Any positive number is greater than any negative number.

rules.title: Difference Rule
rules.text: The difference is the larger number minus the smaller number. It is always positive: difference of -10 and 5 is 5 - (-10) = 15.

rules.title: Equation Rule
rules.text: To solve, undo with the inverse operation on both sides. x + 9 = 17 gives x = 17 - 9 = 8. Check by substituting back.

terms: An approximate answer found by rounding first
A whole number: positive, negative or zero
A number less than zero, with a minus sign
How far apart two numbers are (always positive)
A letter standing for an unknown number
Numbers and letters joined / a number sentence with =

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, copy every worked example onto paper and try it yourself, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, to explain a step in a fresh way, or to check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

I can why does this matter so much? Numbers describe the whole world around you. Traders use large numbers to add up a month of sales. Weather stations use negative numbers to record cold nights on the high plateau near Hargeisa. Banks use negative numbers when an account is overdrawn, and engineers use them for depths below sea level. Algebra – the art of using letters for unknown numbers – is the language that scientists, builders and computer programmers all speak. When you become confident with these ideas, you are gaining tools you will use for the rest of your life, and you are building the strong foundation you need for the harder mathematics ahead.

I can as you work, keep a pencil and plenty of paper beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must do it. Line up the digits, draw the number lines, and say the answers out loud. If you get something wrong, that is not a failure; it is simply a signpost pointing to exactly what to practise next. Every mathematician began where you are now, learning these very same ideas one careful step at a time.
I can estimate sums and differences of large numbers by rounding to a sensible place value.
I can add and subtract large whole numbers accurately using column methods.
I can check calculations using estimation and inverse operations.
I can understand and compare positive and negative numbers on a number line.
I can add and subtract positive and negative integers.
